author: potuz
content: I think the proof can, and should, be against the blockhash that fullfiled it

author: potuz
content: Rather than the beacon block that deducted it

author: potuz
content: There are a few options besides the state, we should really go over all possible scenarios, but blockhashes are referenced in bids from very different blocks

author: potuz
content: so one could use in principle very different blocks from the chain of missing ones to prove against a particular blockhash

author: potuz
content:
So for example, the case that worries <@808969530608451584>. CL N deducts withdrawals. EL N fullfills them, CL N+1 deducts some more. EL N+1 is missing. CL N+2 doesn't deduct anything, EL N+2 comes and blockroot is CL N+1, not CL N. Client wants to prove the withdrawals that were deducted in N and were fullfilled in the same N, but there's no blockroot for N. Proving via the state seems to add 22 hashes, but proving via the parent root of N+1 only adds a couple as far as I can tell.  That is, you provide the blockroot of N and prove that it's the parent root of N+1, then you prove as usual for N. 

This seems to be cheaper than going through the state.
